items.q: What is the difference between brittle and ductile materials?
items.correct: Brittle materials fracture with little or no plastic deformation; ductile materials undergo significant plastic deformation before breaking.
items.misconception: Brittle materials are weak and ductile materials are strong.
items.random_wrong: Brittle materials are metals and ductile materials are non-metals.
items.why: Students conflate brittleness with weakness, missing that a brittle material (like glass) can have very high tensile strength but no warning before failure.

items.q: Was the Big Bang an explosion of matter into pre-existing space?
items.correct: No — it was the expansion of space itself, beginning from a hot dense state; there was no 'outside' for matter to expand into.
items.misconception: Yes — it was a giant explosion at a point, and galaxies are still flying outwards from it.
items.random_wrong: Yes — it was a bomb-like event that destroyed an earlier universe.
items.why: Pop-science imagery of a fiery explosion encourages the wrong mental model; in fact every point in the universe was once 'the centre'.

items.q: How does the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depend on light intensity?
items.correct: It does not — max KE depends only on the frequency of the light; intensity affects the rate (number) of electrons emitted.
items.misconception: Higher intensity gives photoelectrons more kinetic energy.
items.random_wrong: Intensity has no effect on anything in the photoelectric effect.
items.why: Students confuse 'brighter light' with 'more energetic photons'; intensity gives more photons, but each one's energy is set by frequency.

items.q: Force on a current-carrying wire in a B field:
items.why: Correct answer: F = BIL. “F = qE” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: ε = -dΦ/dt. “ε = BIL” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Wire 0.5 m, I = 4 A, B = 0.2 T (perpendicular). Force in N:
items.why: Exact answer: 0.4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
